title: Lesson 374
transcript: You'll remember earlier we were talking about the different generations of programming languages that we've gone through. And really what we're using today is what's called object-oriented programming capabilities. And the word object simply means in this context a piece of code. And we talked about how, you know, today obviously we've evolved in better tool sets to be able to allow us not only to program really good code, but also involve security as part of that. In fact, when you look at you know, some of the capabilities that these entire tool sets have, a lot of these can be useful, very useful from a security perspective. And they include what you see here on this slide. So let me talk about these, like for example, inheritance. Inheritance is the ability of an object, a piece of code, to be able to inherit the characteristics of other objects, pieces of code, that you've previously developed. So the idea behind inheritance is that we can save on programming time because we don't have to keep reprogramming the same characteristics into further objects that we're just creating. We can simply allow those objects to inherit those characteristics automatically. So a really good example of this would be, let's say you've come up with a really good object, a piece of code that does, for example, I don't know, two-factor authentication. Instead of having to reprogram that over and over again into objects that require two-factor authentication, you can simply have those new objects inherit those characteristics. And so that's really good from the perspective of, again, doing programming and developing in a better way, in a more efficient way. What's really important though is to ensure that you're starting with really good characteristics for those objects that are being inherited. Because imagine if you have an object that has vulnerabilities or exploits. If you inherit those characteristics into further objects, then all of those objects are going to have the same exploits and vulnerabilities. So you need to start with something good and have those properties be inherited to further objects. Encapsulation, we've already talked about in the context of, for example, in domain number four, which is all about network security, we talked about encapsulation in the context of tunneling. And we define tunneling as, for example, taking an entire datagram and placing it inside of the data portion of another datagram, right? In other words, we've encapsulated something inside of something else. We can do the same thing here. with objects pieces of code we can take that piece of code that object and encapsulate it within something else either to protect it or to call it within that other object and so that can be useful as well and it's referred to as encapsulation of objects Polymorphism, this is the next one. If you take this word apart, poly means many, morphism means changes. So this is how objects can change based on what they see, for example, in their runtime environments. And in fact, you'll remember Rob talking about polymorphic malware, for example, when he covered this in the malware section. But and that means that, you know, you've got a piece of malware that tries to change its signature basically every time it infects something, right? It's the same concept here. We can have, you know, code or objects that recognizes certain things in its runtime environment and adapt. accordingly. It's almost like smart code that can adapt based on what it sees in its runtime environment. Again, based on us having given it that functionality, it's able to adapt. based on new requirements pretty cool stuff and here's the last one poly instantiation you may remember rob earlier in this course giving you a cliffhanger And I'm going to try and see if you remember where he talked about polyinstantiation. But he would have said something like, and John's going to tell you what polyinstantiation is later in this course. And I'll just give you a couple seconds here to try and think of where that was. All right, if you don't remember, I'll give you the answer to it. He would have been talking about specifically unauthorized inference when he talked about database environments. So this was back in domain number three, where we talked about inherent vulnerabilities that exist in certain architectures. And he would have talked about how one of the problems that we have, especially in database environments, is unauthorized inference. And you have to use the word unauthorized here because inference is actually good. Inference on its own is what we want. And if you think about it, this is why we collect information and store it in databases and update it on a regular basis because we want authorized individuals to be able to look at that information and infer more business decisions, better decisions for example. So inference is good from the perspective of our own organization and those that are authorized. What we don't want is unauthorized inference. Those that don't have a need to know should not be able to infer more sensitive and valuable information. So that's what polyinstantiation can be useful for to prevent unauthorized inference. And again, inference is the ability to deduce more sensitive, more valuable information than someone could. So here's the perfect definition of what polyinstantiation actually means. In other words, if you take the word itself apart, poly means many, instantiation means instances, right? So the definition that you see here allows us really different versions of the same information to exist at different classification levels for the purpose of preventing unauthorized inference. Now, you may be thinking about that and maybe it doesn't quite make sense until I give you this example. I've been using this example for years and people have taken it and used it as well, which I'm totally okay with because as long as it helps you understand and pass the exam. I'm totally fine with that. But here's an example that I've been using for years. And it's, I think, one that carries this concept across really well. But let's say you and I are working in a military environment. and we're maintaining a system. And one day the general comes to us and says, I need you to add a new military unit to our system and call it Fifth Infantry Division or something like that. So we go into the system and try and add a new military unit called 5th Infantry Division. And as soon as we try and do that, the system comes back and gives us an error message. The error message says you cannot add that new military unit called 5th Infantry Division. As soon as we see that error message, we've allowed unauthorized inference because automatically we can infer that there already exists a military unit in the system that's called 5th Infantry Division, right? But we can't see it because it's at a higher level of classification. But now we've allowed inference to happen. We know that there's a 5th Infantry Division that exists in the system. But now we know, but we shouldn't have known. And so if that system had been built using polyinstantiation capabilities, the system would recognize that we are trying to add a new military unit called fifth infantry division that already exists at a higher level of classification than you and i are cleared for so the system would handle it on its own it would say okay whenever John uses 5th Infantry Division at his classification level, I'm going to map it to something else that the system creates on its own. So the system's going to handle it. Every time we or I use 5th Infantry Division at my classification level, the system is going to create some totally different variable or name and map it to whatever, whenever we say for example 5th Infantry Division at our level. In other words, the system's going to handle it on its own without returning a error message that will allow unauthorized inference, right? That's what poly-instantiation allows. And the whole purpose of it is to prevent unauthorized inference by allowing different versions of the same information to exist at different classification levels. Now, before we finish off this section here, we need to talk about something that is mentioned in the exam outline, but also people have been seeing questions on very recently, and that is something called software or code obfuscation. And if you think about that word obfuscation, it means to hide. So the whole purpose of this is to hide certain specific things about code so that you make it very difficult for people to figure out what your code is doing. So think about how a developer develops some code that does something, but they want to be able to protect that code so that it's not stolen or figured out by others. So the idea behind software or code obfuscation is to make it very difficult for someone that's looking at that code to figure out what it does obviously to protect intellectual property and and copyright and all of those things that you'd be interested in protecting if you were developing code all right so the idea behind this is to make it very difficult to reverse engineer and figure out what that code is actually doing. You're concealing or making it very difficult to understand the logic and the purpose of that code that you've produced. Now there's different versions of software or code obfuscation that you see here. There's lexical code obfuscation, there's data code obfuscation, there's also control flow code obfuscation. We've given you the exact definitions here, but the only one that you will see on the exam is the first one, lexical code obfuscation. This is where you're changing the look of the code to again try and make it very difficult for someone to backwards engineer and figure out what your code is doing so in other words you're removing comments You're changing meaningful variable names to non-meaningful ones. You're removing bugging information or, you know, all kinds of things that, again, might give clues as to what that code is doing. So you're removing things that are focused on the look of the code itself so that you're making it very difficult to backwards engineer. That's the whole purpose. of lexical code obfuscation. And just to finish off this section, and this is why, you know, obviously we've been focusing on how security needs to be involved in the entire lifecycle of applications, not just the software development lifecycle. Once we put applications into production, there are a number of things that security needs to be able to support. And we've talked about that many times, but we help in, for example, testing and change management and other things that security needs to be involved in. But the other thing that we need to certainly enforce from a security perspective is that those that are working with development activities, we need to physically and logically divide the environments that they may have access to. This is a form of separation of duties, and I think companies do this pretty well. But for example, If you've got developers that have access to the development environment, they should not have access to the production environment. And they shouldn't have access to the quality assurance environment or the test environment. So through the implementation of security controls. we need to ensure that developers only have access to what they should, based on, again, need to know. Especially dividing your test environment and development environments from production becomes pretty important. and that is something that we always need to enforce. I think companies do this pretty well.
